bio.photo_notes: Henry Winfield Watson, Republican US Congressman from Pennsylvania (1856-1933)

burial.plot_notes: Wilmington and Brandywine Cemetery Wilmington DE per Wikipedia

career.notes: Elected 1914; served nine consecutive Congresses; died in office.

cross_connections.display_label: Both US Congressmen who married into the Hooker-Strong family

narrative_blocks.header: Served nine consecutive Congresses; died in office, 1933
narrative_blocks.body: Henry Winfield Watson was elected to the Sixty-Fourth Congress in 1914 and served nine consecutive terms as Republican representative from Pennsylvania's 8th District, dying in office on August 27, 1933 — having represented Bucks and Montgomery counties for eighteen uninterrupted years. He purchased the Washington, Potomac & Chesapeake Railway in 1909 and was a director of several banks before his congressional career, marrying Cornelia Jerome Winder Ball Watson in 1922.

The Bucks County lawyer purchased the Washington, Potomac & Chesapeake Railway in 1909 and ran it as president while directing several banks — then spent his last eighteen years in Congress, dying in office at seventy-seven.

He purchased the Washington, Potomac & Chesapeake Railway in 1909 and was a director of several banks before his congressional career, marrying Cornelia Jerome Winder Ball Watson in 1922.

burial.plot_notes: Ball Burial Ground Leesburg Loudoun County VA per FA

narrative_blocks.header: First husband Naval captain; second served nine Congresses
narrative_blocks.body: Cornelia Jerome Winder married first Captain Richard Thomson Mason Ball, a US Navy paymaster who died in 1913 leaving her with a daughter Janet; then in November 1922 she married Henry Winfield Watson, a Republican Congressman from Pennsylvania then in his fifth term, who served nine consecutive terms in total and died in office in 1933. Her first husband's Ball family had deep Confederate Virginia roots — his father Captain George Washington Ball CSA had founded a school for Confederate orphans near Leesburg after the war.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)
